title: Defense-in-Depth for GenAI
description:
## Layered Responsible-AI Controls

An architect must present a **responsible-AI control design** for a regulated, agentic Bedrock application. Which combination of controls best reflects defense-in-depth across **identity, content safety, oversight, and auditability**?

options.text: AgentCore Identity for scoped agent access, Bedrock Guardrails for content/PII policy, Step Functions for human approval of high-impact actions, and model-invocation logging for audit
A single broad IAM role for the agent and trust in the model to behave correctly
Only enable Guardrails; identity and oversight are unnecessary if content is filtered
Disable logging to avoid storing any sensitive prompts, and skip approvals to reduce latency

explanation: Responsible-AI design is layered: scope the agent's identity and permissions (AgentCore Identity), enforce content and PII policy at the model boundary (Bedrock Guardrails), gate high-impact actions behind human approval (Step Functions), and keep an audit trail (model-invocation logging). Each layer addresses a different failure mode. A single broad role and blind trust (b) ignores least privilege and safety. Guardrails alone (c) leaves identity and oversight gaps. Disabling logging and approvals (d) trades away auditability and governance — the opposite of what a regulated workload needs.

hints.title: Each control covers a different risk
hints.body: Identity limits what the agent can do, guardrails limit what it can say, oversight limits what executes, and logging proves what happened.
